reader_guide: Start with `refactor_targets`: those are the deduplicated engineering moves behind the per-dimension grades. Prioritize Medium severity with High confidence first because those findings are directly source-backed and have clear blast radius. Medium confidence findings still matter, but they need one targeted runtime or unit reproduction before they become merge-blocking work. The three Static Risk dimensions are provisional by definition: performance, lifecycle, and security posture require runtime validation with Instruments, heart-path benchmarks, network inspection, and release-build checks. Concurrency claims are intentionally conservative in this report; I found strong positive concurrency patterns and did not promote a race claim without a concrete interleaving. The phased roadmap is dependency-ordered, not preference-ordered: Phase 1 contains changes that stand alone, later phases depend on those surfaces being narrowed first. Treat clean dimensions as “no source-proven violation found in this static pass,” not as proof that runtime behavior is perfect.

overall_grade.rationale: This is shippable product engineering with real architecture, real isolation work, and several senior patterns. It is not AI slop, but it still carries central-coordinator debt, broad mutable API exposure, and a few failure-path gaps that deserve tracked cleanup.
overall_grade.confidence_rationale: I read the rubric files, module map, pipeline mechanics, package graph, source inventory, high-risk source paths, and sampled tests for coverage evidence. I did not run the app, benchmarks, tests, Instruments, or CI because this audit was static and the sandbox is read-only.

dimensions.name: Architecture integrity
dimensions.justification: The package graph is clean and the dependency-direction script reports no module-direction violations. The main architectural debt is still the app shell: `AppState` imports almost every product module and owns settings, permissions, audio, ASR, keychain, hotkeys, benchmark, overlay, setup, telemetry, both pipelines, transcript history, custom words, discovery, and AI availability. This is not a broken design, but it is still a central coordination mass that the project’s own anti-god-object rule warns against.

best_example.why_exemplary: The pipeline target depends downward through named modules instead of importing app/view state, and the local dependency-direction script returned clean across all modules.

worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The stored properties and imports show `AppState` directly owning many unrelated domains. The architecture knowledge file also names `AppState` as current debt, but this finding stands on the source lines.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Keep `AppState` as the composition root only.
Move hotkey-flow decisions into a recording coordinator.
Move display-only derived state into view-facing adapters.
Keep pipeline ownership and settings fanout behind narrow methods.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: This follows the existing extracted-coordinator pattern visible in `Sources/EnviousWispr/App/PipelineSettingsSync.swift:L9-L13`, where one narrow owner handles one live settings concern.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Add a new per-recording setting that changes paste behavior. If implementation requires edits in `AppState`, `PipelineSettingsSync`, and both pipeline files instead of a single local owner plus tests, the centralization debt is exposed.

dimensions.name: Concurrency discipline
dimensions.justification: The codebase shows deliberate Swift 6 isolation work: audio/XPC callbacks hop back to the main actor, pre-roll avoids callbacks under locks, and async recovery paths use token gates. I did not find a source-proven concurrency bug with a defensible interleaving in this static pass. Manual re-verification is still needed around hotkey cancellation and XPC callback timing because those are runtime interleavings.

best_example.why_exemplary: This is the right shape for audio-thread code: bounded lock work, no callback execution inside the lock, and a comment that states the real-time constraint plainly.

worst_violation.rule_violated: No violation found in this dimension.
worst_violation.confidence_rationale: I inspected high-risk concurrency patterns including audio-thread forwarding, XPC nonisolated handlers, `Task` usage, `OSAllocatedUnfairLock`, and actor isolation. I did not find a race claim that met the required concrete-interleaving bar.
worst_violation.counterfactual: No violation found in this dimension.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: No violation found in this dimension.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Run a stress test that alternates PTT press/release, backend switch, and XPC service kill while recording 100 times; a real concurrency defect would show stuck recording state, duplicate stop, missing paste, or leaked capture.

dimensions.name: Error handling & observability
dimensions.justification: The codebase has strong observability wrappers, Sentry redaction, structured pipeline breadcrumbs, and explicit heart-path error categories. The weak spot is that app-level heart controls still erase thrown pipeline errors with `try?` in several user-action paths. That is a contract smell: the protocol was widened to surface failures, but the caller discards them.

best_example.snippet:
public static func captureError(
    _ error: any Error,
    category: ErrorCategory,
    stage: String,
    extra: [String: Any]? = nil,
best_example.why_exemplary: Handled errors flow through one typed helper with category, stage, extras, and optional recording snapshot instead of scattered ad hoc logging.

worst_violation.rule_violated: .claude/rules/architecture-rules.md §Heart & Limbs and .claude/rules/validation-discipline.md §5 Validate automated review findings against actual code
worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The source proves the error is discarded. Current concrete pipeline implementations do not throw on `.toggleRecording`, so the current runtime defect requires a throwing implementation or later event change.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Replace `try?` with `do/catch` at AppState heart-control boundaries.
On error, hide recording UI, clear lock state, call `setExternalError`, and emit a typed Sentry category.
Add one fake-pipeline unit test that throws on toggle.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: This matches the existing explicit preWarm handling in `Sources/EnviousWispr/App/AppState.swift:L532-L557`, where errors abort start and surface a user-visible failure.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Inject a fake `DictationPipeline` whose `handle(.toggleRecording)` throws `XPCTransportError.serviceUnreachable`; call `toggleRecording()`, then assert the overlay is hidden, lock state is false, and a pipeline error is visible instead of a silent no-op.

dimensions.name: Testability
dimensions.justification: There is meaningful testable design: injected log sinks, internal test initializers, fakeable protocols, and architecture ceiling tests. The main weakness is global persistence in `SettingsManager`, where every property writes directly to `UserDefaults.standard` and fires synchronous callbacks. That makes isolated settings tests and state-reset discipline harder than needed.

best_example.why_exemplary: The runner separates policy from string matching and injects logging, which lets tests verify behavior without reading debug logs.

worst_violation.rule_violated: .claude/rules/validation-discipline.md §7 Use existing harness first and testability split principle
worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The snippet shows direct global persistence in a setter. This is a testability issue rather than a user-facing defect.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Inject a small settings store abstraction or `UserDefaults` instance.
Keep production defaulting to `.standard`.
Give tests a suite-backed store and explicit reset.
Keep `onChange` behavior unchanged.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: This mirrors `TranscriptStore` keeping production defaults while exposing an internal directory initializer for tests in `Sources/EnviousWisprStorage/TranscriptStore.swift:L24-L35`.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Run two settings tests in randomized order where one changes `selectedBackend` and another asserts the launch default. Without a suite-scoped store and reset, order dependence appears through `UserDefaults.standard`.

dimensions.name: Code Hygiene & Maintainability
dimensions.justification: The code contains many thoughtful local extractions, but several production files remain too large for easy review and change safety. The largest files include two 1,300-line pipelines, a 954-line app state, and 1,000-line SwiftUI views. The maintainability issue is not style; it is the cost of proving changes safe in files that mix orchestration, state, telemetry, and UI glue.

best_example.why_exemplary: This extraction names the ordering contract and makes the wiring testable instead of burying it in `AppState`.

worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The source shows `AppState` still owns both concrete pipelines and cross-pipeline state-change behavior. File inventory also shows `AppState.swift` at 954 lines.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Extract recording-session command handling from `AppState`.
Keep only construction and user-facing derived state in `AppState`.
Move cross-pipeline warning and hotkey ordering into a focused owner with tests.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: The existing `CustomWordsPropagator` pattern at `Sources/EnviousWispr/App/CustomWordsPropagator.swift:L63-L75` shows how ordering-sensitive AppState wiring can be extracted safely.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Ask a new engineer to change post-completion warning timing. If they need to reason through `AppState`, both pipelines, overlay, and telemetry rather than one focused owner plus tests, the maintainability violation is active.

dimensions.name: API surface
dimensions.justification: The codebase uses real modules, but the cross-module API surface is still wider than ideal. I counted 833 `public` or `package` declarations across the non-app modules, and some of that exposure leaks implementation objects instead of narrow commands. The clearest case is `TranscriptPolishService` exposing its mutable `LLMPolishStep` so app-level settings code can reach through and mutate internals.

best_example.why_exemplary: This is narrow visibility with an explicit test seam: production cannot misuse the alternate initializer, but tests still get control.

worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The snippet exposes a mutable implementation object publicly, and `PipelineSettingsSync` directly mutates its properties.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Make `llmPolishStep` private.
Expose `applySettingsSnapshot(_:)` or `configurePolisher(provider:model:instructions:style:thinking:)`.
Keep request-time snapshotting inside `TranscriptPolishService`.
Test that settings changes update re-polish behavior through the narrow method.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: This follows the access-control rule and matches the narrow test-only initializer pattern in `Sources/EnviousWisprStorage/TranscriptStore.swift:L24-L35`.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Create a caller in another module that sets `polishService.llmPolishStep.onWillProcess` or changes `backend` mid-enhancement; the current public surface permits that misuse.

dimensions.name: Performance & latency (Static Risk Assessment)
dimensions.justification: Static code shows serious attention to hot-path latency: pre-roll is preallocated, ASR runs in XPC, streaming rescue exists, and the app avoids callbacks under audio locks. The main static risk is the LLM prewarm path: it sends real one-token inference requests on launch, foreground activation, and while ASR runs after capture. Runtime benchmarks and provider logs are required to decide whether this is a net win or wasted network/model work.

best_example.why_exemplary: This protects latency and correctness by trying the fast streaming path while preserving a batch rescue path for heart completion.

worst_violation.rule_violated: .claude/rules/architecture-rules.md §Heart & Limbs limb resource constraint and .claude/rules/validation-discipline.md §9 Test the cold path
worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The source proves real inference prewarm exists and is called on user flows. Runtime provider metrics are required to prove net latency impact.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Add a per-provider prewarm rate limiter and in-flight coalescing.
Skip prewarm when the provider/model has warmed recently.
Record token cost, status, and effect on the following polish call.
Disable prewarm automatically when it fails or adds no measured benefit.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: This applies the cold-path measurement rule in `validation-discipline.md §9` and keeps the limb from consuming resources without evidence.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Configure OpenAI polish, launch the app, foreground it five times, then dictate three times. Provider logs should show whether eight one-token warmup calls occurred and whether the next polish latency improved versus a no-prewarm control.

dimensions.name: Resource lifecycle (Static Risk Assessment)
dimensions.justification: Most resource lifecycles are explicitly handled: hotkeys unregister on termination, XPC invalidation paths nil connections, audio sources cancel work items, and pipelines cancel VAD workers. One concrete leak path remains in the ASR XPC progress polling path: an error thrown during model load skips `stopProgressPolling()`. This is a static resource risk that a targeted fake-XPC test can confirm.

best_example.why_exemplary: Termination explicitly removes observers and later stops hotkeys and invalidates the shared LLM network session, which is the right lifecycle shape for a menu-bar app.

worst_violation.rule_violated: .claude/rules/architecture-rules.md §Audio/ASR Danger Zones and .claude/rules/validation-discipline.md §8 No blame toolchain
worst_violation.confidence_rationale: The function starts a repeating timer before an awaited throwing operation and only stops polling after that operation succeeds. No `defer` exists inside the task.
worst_violation.counterfactual:
Add `defer { self.stopProgressPolling() }` immediately after `startProgressPolling()`.
Clear progress state in both success and failure branches.
Add a fake service test that returns an error from `loadModel`.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: This is the same cleanup-on-all-paths discipline already used by `TranscriptStore.save` cleanup in `Sources/EnviousWisprStorage/TranscriptStore.swift:L62-L65`.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Stub the ASR XPC service so `loadModel` calls its completion with an error. After `loadModel()` throws, assert `progressPollTimer` is nil or observe that progress polling keeps firing at 4 Hz.

dimensions.name: Security & privacy (Static Risk Assessment)
dimensions.justification: The static privacy posture is stronger than average for a dictation app: transcript files are owner-only, Spotlight indexing is disabled, Sentry/PostHog redaction exists, and telemetry uses counts rather than transcript text. I found no source-proven shipped-release privacy leak in this pass. This remains a static judgment; release-build log behavior and telemetry payloads need external verification.

best_example.why_exemplary: The storage layer states and implements concrete transcript privacy controls: owner-only directory, owner-only files, and Spotlight exclusion.

worst_violation.rule_violated: No violation found in this dimension.
worst_violation.confidence_rationale: I inspected Sentry redaction, transcript storage, key handling call sites, and telemetry search results. I found no source-backed shipped-release privacy leak.
worst_violation.counterfactual: No violation found in this dimension.
worst_violation.counterfactual_rationale: No violation found in this dimension.
worst_violation.falsifiability: Run a release build with a canary transcript containing an email address, API-key-shaped string, and long personal sentence; trigger Sentry and PostHog events, then verify no payload contains the canary.

refactor_targets.title: Continue AppState decomposition into focused recording and display owners
refactor_targets.dimension: Architecture integrity; Code Hygiene & Maintainability; Testability
refactor_targets.fix_shape: Move recording command orchestration, cross-pipeline warning timing, and display-state derivation out of `AppState` into one or two focused owners. Keep construction in `AppState`, but make behavior changes land in smaller tested files.

refactor_targets.title: Hide TranscriptPolishService internals behind a narrow configuration API
refactor_targets.fix_shape: Make `llmPolishStep` private and expose a method that applies the few settings the re-polish service needs. Keep snapshot creation inside `TranscriptPolishService` so other modules cannot mutate callbacks or backend routing.

refactor_targets.title: Replace AppState heart-control try? calls with explicit error handling
refactor_targets.fix_shape: Wrap `handle(event:)` calls in `do/catch` at AppState heart-control boundaries. On failure, clear transient UI state, surface a pipeline error, and emit a typed breadcrumb.

refactor_targets.title: Guarantee ASR XPC progress polling cleanup on model-load failure
refactor_targets.dimension: Resource lifecycle (Static Risk Assessment); Performance & latency (Static Risk Assessment); Error handling & observability
refactor_targets.fix_shape: Add a `defer` around `startProgressPolling()` in `ASRManagerProxy.loadModel()`. Cover the throwing XPC completion path with a fake service or injectable polling hook.

refactor_targets.title: Rate-limit and measure real-inference LLM prewarm
refactor_targets.dimension: Performance & latency (Static Risk Assessment); Resource lifecycle (Static Risk Assessment)
refactor_targets.fix_shape: Add in-flight coalescing, a recent-warm timestamp, and telemetry that links prewarm to the next polish call. Keep the feature only if cold-path measurements show user-visible benefit.

refactor_targets.title: Inject settings persistence instead of hard-wiring UserDefaults.standard
refactor_targets.fix_shape: Give `SettingsManager` a store or `UserDefaults` dependency with `.standard` as the production default. Tests get a suite-backed store and explicit reset while production behavior remains unchanged.

phased_roadmap.title: Small Correctness Fixes
phased_roadmap.rationale: These fixes are local, have no dependency on broader architecture work, and close concrete failure-path gaps first.

phased_roadmap.title: Narrow Mutable Surfaces
phased_roadmap.rationale: Narrowing the re-polish API and settings persistence reduces cross-module mutation before larger decomposition starts.

phased_roadmap.title: Measure Runtime Risk
phased_roadmap.rationale: LLM prewarm policy needs measurement after the easy lifecycle and API fixes are out of the way. The output is a keep, throttle, or remove decision backed by provider and latency data.

phased_roadmap.title: Decompose AppState
phased_roadmap.rationale: The large coordinator refactor benefits from the narrower APIs created earlier, which lowers the chance that decomposition creates another broad manager.

strengths.why_preserve: The code explicitly limits lock work and runs callbacks outside the lock; this is the right pattern for real-time audio paths. Snippet: `Lock hold time is minimal... Callbacks are invoked OUTSIDE the lock.`

strengths.pattern_name: Heart continues when limb processing fails
strengths.why_preserve: Text-processing failures log and continue with input text, preserving raw transcription delivery. Snippet: `catch { ... "skipping" ... // Heart & Limbs: limb failed, continue with input text`.

Transcript writes use an owner-only temp file before rename, avoiding a permissions window around private dictation text. Snippet: `Writes to a temp file at 0600 first... avoids the brief world-readable window`.
The pipeline target depends on lower-level modules and no longer imports WhisperKit directly. This matches the architecture map and keeps ASR internals contained.

meta_recommendations.recommendation: Add a lightweight API-surface report to CI that counts `public` and `package` declarations by module and flags new public mutable implementation objects.
meta_recommendations.triggered_by: REF-02 and the 833 cross-module public/package declarations found by static search.

meta_recommendations.recommendation: Add one failure-path unit test family for app-level heart controls using fake `DictationPipeline` implementations that throw on each event.
meta_recommendations.triggered_by: REF-03: `AppState` discards `handle(event:)` errors even though the protocol is throwing.

meta_recommendations.recommendation: Add a fake-XPC lifecycle test for ASR model-load failure paths, including timer cleanup and progress reset.
meta_recommendations.triggered_by: REF-04: `ASRManagerProxy.loadModel()` starts polling before a throwing continuation and stops only after success.

not_assessed.area: End-to-end heart-path completion on real macOS
not_assessed.reason: Static source review cannot prove audio capture, ASR, paste, permissions, and target-app focus work together at runtime.
not_assessed.how_to_cover: Run `scripts/heart-path-bench.sh --cold`, `scripts/heart-path-check.sh`, and Live UAT through `Tests/RuntimeUAT/wispr_eyes.py` on the signed app.

not_assessed.area: Performance and latency impact of LLM prewarm
not_assessed.reason: Source proves real inference requests are sent, but source cannot prove latency benefit, token cost, provider throttling, or network contention.
not_assessed.how_to_cover: Run A/B cold-path benchmarks with prewarm enabled and disabled, capture provider request logs, and compare next-polish p50/p95 latency.

not_assessed.reason: Static review identifies lifecycle suspects but cannot measure retained ASR models, timers, XPC connections, or URLSession tasks.
not_assessed.how_to_cover: Use Instruments Allocations/Leaks plus repeated backend switch, model load failure, dictation, and app foreground cycles.

not_assessed.reason: Static review found redaction and release logging gates, but payload contents require observing actual Sentry/PostHog events from a release build.
not_assessed.how_to_cover: Use canary transcript strings and inspect Sentry/PostHog payloads from a signed release build with production configuration.

not_assessed.area: Concurrency interleavings around hotkeys and XPC callbacks
not_assessed.reason: The code contains strong guards, but static review cannot exhaust event timing between key press/release, XPC interruption, backend switch, and audio callbacks.
not_assessed.how_to_cover: Run a stress harness that randomizes PTT timing, service kills, backend switches, and device route changes while asserting no stuck recording state or missing paste.

not_assessed.reason: I ran the local dependency-direction script, but I did not query GitHub Actions or run the full test suite.
not_assessed.how_to_cover: Run `scripts/validate-pr.sh` or the project’s full CI lane and inspect architecture tests under `Tests/EnviousWisprTests/Architecture/`.

least_confident_finding.refactor_id_or_description: REF-05: real-inference LLM prewarm as a performance risk
least_confident_finding.why_low_confidence: The source proves warmup calls exist, but only runtime A/B data can show whether they improve or harm real dictation latency.
least_confident_finding.what_would_resolve_it: A cold-path benchmark comparing prewarm enabled versus disabled, with provider request counts and next-polish latency tied to the same sessions.

top_not_assessed_gap.gap: End-to-end heart-path runtime behavior across real audio, ASR, paste, permissions, and target-app focus.
top_not_assessed_gap.runtime_test_required: Run signed-app Live UAT using real microphone/speaker input across Parakeet and WhisperKit, with paste verification and logs captured for every stage.
top_not_assessed_gap.why_this_matters_for_heart_limbs: Heart/Limbs is a runtime doctrine: the code can look well layered while a permission prompt, XPC interruption, model load, or paste fallback still blocks the user from receiving raw text.
